coredata.dc:title: Emerging hybrid biomaterials for oxidative stress induced photodynamic therapy

Solanki, Archana

coredata.dc:description: Cancer therapy has undergone tremendous advancements in the past few years. The drawbacks of most of these therapies have encouraged researchers to obtain further insight into the complex chemical, biochemical and biological processes ongoing in the evolving cancer cells. These studies have led to an advent of reactive oxygen species mediated therapies to target and disrupt the cancer pathology. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has emerged as a potent candidate for oxidative stress mediated non-invasive technique for rapid diagnosis and treatment of cancer. Towards this, biomacromolecules derived hybrid nanomaterials have contributed largely in the development of various therapeutics and theranostics for efficacious cancer management that can assist PDT. This review summarizes various hybrid biomaterials and advanced techniques that have been explored widely in the past few years for PDT application. The article also mentions some of the important in-vitro and in-vivo developments and observations explored by employing these materials for PDT application. The article also describes the interactions of these materials at the biological interface and the probable mechanism that assist in generation of oxidative stress and subsequent cell death.

head.ce:title: Emerging hybrid biomaterials for oxidative stress induced photodynamic therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cancer therapy has undergone tremendous advancements in the past few years. The drawbacks of most of these therapies have encouraged researchers to obtain further insight into the complex chemical, biochemical and biological processes ongoing in the evolving cancer cells. These studies have led to an advent of reactive oxygen species mediated therapies to target and disrupt the cancer pathology. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has emerged as a potent candidate for oxidative stress mediated non-invasive technique for rapid diagnosis and treatment of cancer. Towards this, biomacromolecules derived hybrid nanomaterials have contributed largely in the development of various therapeutics and theranostics for efficacious cancer management that can assist PDT. This review summarizes various hybrid biomaterials and advanced techniques that have been explored widely in the past few years for PDT application. The article also mentions some of the important in-vitro and in-vivo developments and observations explored by employing these materials for PDT application. The article also describes the interactions of these materials at the biological interface and the probable mechanism that assist in generation of oxidative stress and subsequent cell death.

ce:section.ce:para:
Generation of oxidative stress in a cell is a condition wherein there is an excess of reactive oxygen species (ROS) as compared to the antioxidants. The cell bears various complex biochemical mechanisms to establish a harmony between the two species. However an imbalance is associated with certain physiological anomalies. The same is true for cancer wherein ROS is associated with tumorigenesis and ample amounts of ROS generated within the tumor microenvironment that turns lethal for the cancer cells [1]. There are various ways to generate an oxidative stress within tumor and Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has evolved as an effective and practical theranostic strategy for the same. The treatment regimen is non-invasive in nature and depends on the administration of a light sensitive singlet oxygen species generating molecule called photosensitizer (PS). The PS accumulates within the tumor tissues and is only activated to release ROS in presence of endogenous molecular oxygen when irradiated with light source of appropriate wavelength. The light source is assorted as per the type of PS involved, the dosage of light required and the site of tumor. A variety of ROS are generated in the process, for instance singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anion (O2
−) and hydroxyl radicals (
OH). The ROS thus produced is highly toxic causing tumor cell death via oxidative stress. The phenomenon can be simply understood with the help of a Jablonski diagram as shown in Fig. 1 c.

It is noteworthy that the cytotoxic species are released only upon irradiation by light otherwise the PSs are minimally toxic and hence are not a threat to normal cells [2]. Thus PDT is a modality that is comparatively specific poses lower side effects as compared to other light based modalities like radiotherapy.

Despite the potential of PDT for inducing an effective oxidative stress for efficient cancer cell death, the treatment regimen faces some clinical interventions due to certain limitations. The commonly faced hindrances include the challenges in formulating an ideal PS, choosing the perfect light dosage for an effective treatment and difficulties in administration of the treatment [3]. The development of hybrid nanomaterials as therapeutic and theranostic devices for PDT applications has been an effective solution towards overcoming many of these obstacles. This paper attempts to summarize some of the hybrid biomaterials developed in the past few years for applications in PDT along with the mechanism involved in generation of an oxidative stress for cell death. Some advanced techniques, adapted recently for improvising the PDT for better outcomes, have also been described.
Researchers have worked extensively over the past few decades towards the development of nanomaterials based Photodynamic systems (Fig. 1a).

Such systems serve as novel modalities wherein the biological macromolecule derived nanomaterials have been explored either as a carrier of photosensitizer (PS) or as energy transducers of PS and even solitarily as PS itself. For instance Xiong et al. developed a new photosensitizer, which can be used against both cancer cell proliferation and rapid bacterial activation [6]. They designed two aggregation-induced emission luminogens with a strong electron push − pull effect. The two mitochondria targeting AIEgens exhibit a highly efficient photodynamic ablation of HeLa cells as well as an excellent photodynamic inactivation to both Gram-positive S. Aureus and Gram-negative E. coli. The authors have used time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) to investigate excitation of the photo-sensitisers to triplet state. The effect of iodide anions on photodynamic efficacy of PS is also demonstrated. This is due to the stability of these materials under irradiation and their superior optical properties which aids enhanced penetration within tissues [3]& [4] (Fig. 1b). These unique properties have caused an inclination towards application of nanomaterial mediated PDT over conventional PDT using organic PS. The generation of reactive oxygen species occurs as per the Jablonski diagram as shown in (Fig. 1c).

Research on metal nanoparticle based PDT proves that a nanoparticle mediated targeted delivery approach is promising as it enhances tumor-specific accumulation of photosensitizer. This not only reduces the toxicity of the material being used but also decreases the dose required [7]. Literature reports points towards a use of large number of metal nanoparticles and metal derived nanocomposites for PDT applications. Gold and silver nanoparticles are widely preferred materials for this form of cancer therapy due to their ability to induce oxidative stress, amplified specificity and negligible risk of side effects. Both these nanoparticles have the ability to generate morphology depended singlet oxygen species [8]. For instance it was observed that the excitation of silver decahedrons and silver triangular nanoplates results in the production of singlet oxygen whereas there is little or no 1O2 signal was observed when silver nanocubes are used [9]. Another important factor to be considered while tuning these nanoparticles for PDT application is that the radiation wavelength should differ from their localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) wavelength of efficient sensitization and singlet oxygen species release. This has been proven experimentally by Hwang and co-workers, who successfully achieved a significant 1O2 formation by photoexciting silver decahedrons at 885 nm NIR wavelength which is different from LSPR band (520 nm) of silver [10]. Other that Au and Ag, iron oxide nanoparticles have been used in this application as well. A conjugate of curcumin with superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticle has exhibited heating abilities under the influence of an alternating magnetic field (AMF) and photodynamic potential upon blue LED light irradiation. Interestingly, Cu nanoparticles have also been explored recently for their application in PDT in the form of Cu-cysteamine complex [11]. A system designed by combination of copper-cysteamine nanoparticles and potassium iodide (KI) was tested for its potent PDT function [12]. Upon dosage to Gram-positive MRSA and Gram-negative E. coli, the nanoparticles significantly caused an inhibition to both the strands of bacteria. A mechanistic probing into this phenomenon presented the findings that copper ions were not released after adding KI to nanoparticles in cell-free medium. Thus it was confirmed that the bacterial killing is not caused by Cu ions induced Fenton reaction. The researchers thus arrived at a conclusion that there is a generation of toxic species, such as hydrogen peroxide, triiodide ions, iodide ions, singlet oxygen, and iodine molecules under UV light in the presence of these nanoparticles that cause death of bacteria. This presents the potency of Copper-cysteamine nanoparticles along with KI as a photosensitizer that can be employed in PDT [12]. Moreover Chen and co-workers have established that Cu-cysteamine nanoparticles upon activation with UV, microwave as well as X-rays are capable of generating ROS [13]. The in-vivo results show a statistically significant reduction in tumor size under X-ray activation of pH-low insertion peptide–conjugated, copper-cysteamine nanoparticles in mouse tumors. This work confirms the effectiveness of copper-cysteamine nanoparticles as a photosensitizer when activated by radiation and suggests that these Cu-Cy nanoparticles may be good candidates for PDT in deeply seated tumors when combined with X-rays and conjugated to a tumor targeting molecule [14–17].

A new class of compounds called metal organic framework nanoparticles is being explored recently as photosensitizers for PDT. They are new generation, hybrid porous nanomaterials having dual combination of inorganic building blocks and organic linkers. A porphyrin containing Zr–metal–organic framework nanoparticle was synthesized by Min et al. as a photosensitizer and delivery vehicle of vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 2 (VEGFR2) for tumor inhibition. The intravenous administration of this formulation causes its selective accumulation in the tumor and the combination of enhanced PDT and antiangiogenic drug causes significant cell death [7].

The development of 2D nanomaterials has undergone exceptional scientific and technological advances. They are thin sheet like materials having lateral size of > 100 nm to few μm and with thickness of few atoms (< 5 nm) have unique chemical and physical properties well suited for PDT applications [18]. For instance, their physical features enable them to convert light to heat in a much efficient manner as compared to other nanomaterials [4]. Therefore, 2D nanomaterials acting as PSs not only share the same advantages as the other types of nanomaterials, but also offer some new benefits to further improve the therapeutic efficacy at the same time. The limitations of low selectivity and poor water solubility faced by conventional photosensitizers can be resolved using 2D nanomaterials. For this various nanomaterials which can intrinsically generate ROS upon light irradiation like graphene quantum dots, porous silicon, ZnO and TiO2 NPs are incorporated within 2D materials. Various 2D materials widely explored for the application are metal based nanosheets, graphene oxide, phosphorous based compounds etc. Titanium has been established as a biocompatible material as various 2D Ti nanosheets have been successfully employed as theranostic devices for image guided photothermal therapy [19]. Taking inspiration from this, researchers have modified Ti nanosheets to suit PDT application (Fig. 1b). For overcoming the issues of biocompatibility, developments have been made to synthesize biomaterials derived or functionalized 2D systems for PDT applications. For instance Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) having biogenic origin like curcumin derived GQDs have been applied for drug delivery and as excellent candidates for PDT owing to their up-conversion characteristics [20]. Moreover natural polymers like dextran, cellulose, chitosan etc have been used to improvise the hydrophilicity and biocompatibility as coating materials for better application in PDT applications [21].

The properties of high emission quantum yield, emission that can be tuned by size and composition and facile surface functionalization possessed by Quantum dots (QDs) make them promising antioxidants or pro-oxidants for inducing oxidative stress in organisms. Various advances have taken place in synthesis of QDs post their widely known applications for this therapy. Prior to 2003 semiconductor QDs were largely employed in biomedicine for imaging applications; however Burda and co-workers in 2003 emerged with the concept of combining semiconductor QDs with photosensitizers. The efficiency of QDs gets enhanced by doping them with appropriate photosensitizers. Chlorine-doped graphene QDs with variable Cl doping were prepared to obtain about 7-fold improvised free radical and 1O2 production efficiency as compared to un-doped QDs [22]. Improvements in synthesis strategies have helped to prepare QDs via one pot hydrothermal methods. Researchers have successfully synthesized multifunctional QDs with targeting abilities by introducing rationalized functional groups. Manganese-doped carbon quantum dots (Mn-CQDs) have been prepared in such a way that the QDs have amine functionalization. This facilitates the ease of folic acid conjugation to the Mn-CQDs which introduces targeting ability in the material. The incorporation of chlorin e6 ensures that the final material is capable of inducing PDT [23]. In addition to this, the Mn2+ added as dopant imparts magnetic resonating capability to the QDs. Thus the QDs are magneto-fluorescent and can induce oxidative stress upon suitable irradiation. Similar folate targeting QDs have been explored for PDT applications by encapsulating other photosensitizers like Rhodamine 6 G and NIR775 via encapsulation phospholipid polymers [10].

Mesoporous silica-based nanoparticles (MSNPs) belong to group of nanomaterials with porous structure comprised of honey-comb like arrangement of several empty channels [24]. These pores provide opportunities for encapsulation of bioactive molecules including drugs and phototherapeutic agents [25]. MSNPs are explored in recent biomedical research due to its excellent properties including tailored pore size, biocompatibility, tunable particle size, greater surface area, non-toxicity and facile surface functionalization [26].

The MSNPs are attractive candidates for encapsulation of PS both by absorption and covalent linkage. The later is a more recent technique, which is also effective in preventing premature release of PS from the MSNPs [27]. A variety of PS including PpIX, Pd-mesotetra(4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin, aluminium phthalocyaninedisulfonate, and zinc(II) phthalocyanine have been reported to be embedded in MSNPs to improve its PDT effects [28]. Moreover, poor water solubility and insufficient availability of PS can be addressed by bonding them with MSNPs. The tumor uptake of MSNPs can be improved by attaching biomolecule group on its surface such that it can target neoplastic cell membranes. These are also called third generation nanovectors. Such nanovectors hence possess valuable properties for PDT including strongly attached PS group and targeting cancer cells with biomolecule. Owing to high surface area and suitable pore volume, MSNPs have been utilized for fabrication of multifunctional nanoplatform where PS like rose bengal (RB) and carbon dots were embedded in the core/shell structured MSNPs (Fig. 1) [29]. The resulting MSN@C-dots/RB nanoparticles yielded fluorescence probe offered by carbon dots, and effective PDT due to rose bengal. The self aggregation induced quenching of PS could be prevented due to MSNPs which resulted in effective production of cytotosic singlet oxygen and thus an enhanced PDT.

Core-shell hybrid nanoparticles (SNPs) were synthesized with silica core and xylan carrying 5-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-10,15,20-triphenylporphyrin (TPPOH) shell for PDT. The conjugation of TPPOH to SNPs resulted into numerous advantages including enhanced solubility and uptake by cancer cells. This resulted into efficient cancer cell killing, which was confirmed by monitoring cell death using transmission electron microscopy. In another report, hollow MSNPs were conjugated with chitosan as a capping layer, along with folic acid, pheophorbide and doxorubicin as targeting agent, photosensitizer and antineoplastic drug respectilvely [30]. This assembly was claimed to possess pH-dependent drug delivery efficiency as well as excellent capability for photothermal-photodynamic therapies. In another report Gary-Bobo et al. employed mannose functionalized MSNPs loaded with several PSs in order to obtain tumor specific targeting [31]. MSNPs are reported to prevent unwanted excitation of PS by UV/visible light, along with selectivity to tumor cite and enhanced efficiency. Also, small particle size and high pore volume of MSNPs is attributed to high PS loading capacity leading to PDT efficiency.

Polymers have been employed in PDT as carriers of photosensitisers, with or without associated through vascular and anti angiogenic agents in order to confer additional advantages [32]. For example, the limitation of photosensitizers lies in their poor water solubility and inadequate disruption of outer membrane of Gram negative bacteria. To address this, Karimi et al. reported incorporation of zinc phthalocyanine-colistin (ZnPc-Col) conjugate into chitosan hydrogel [33]. The pore size of the hydrogel, viscoelastic properties, and singlet oxygen production efficiency were tuned by varying degree of crosslinking of ZnPc-Col with glutaraldehyde. Zinc phthalocyanine showed enhanced 1O2 production potency due to prevention of aggregation and also increased permeability of phthalocyanine on outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria. A biopolymer like Hyaluronic acid has been linked with chlorine e6 using adipic dihydrazide as a linker to sustain PDT and chemotherapy [34]. It was claimed that the resulting nanoparticles offered enhanced DOX delivery and improved cellular uptake efficiency in A549 cells. This could increase efficiency of chemotherapy and photodynamic therapy.

Dendrimers are important class of materials in the field of biological sciences due to its typical physical architecture, provision of large internal free volume and adaptability towards placement of various functional groups. The possibility of incorporation of photosensitizers (PS) in the dendrimer core, surface groups or at internal cavities between branches has enabled its application for PDT [35]. It is noteworthy that the efficiency or spectral properties of PS is not altered even upon encapsulation or conjugation with dendrimers. In certain systems the encapsulation of PS within a dendritic core helped to enhance the triplet decay time that turned out to be beneficial for PDT. The surface charges on dendrimers also play a role in the formation of oxygen species to cytotoxicity enhancement. Dendrimers have been reported to strengthen the PDT applications by prevailing over its limitation of reduced efficacy due to hypoxia in tumor microenvironment (TME) [36]. This can be explained as follows. The amino groups of dendrimers are known for its strong coordination with metal ions. This property is beneficial for PDT applications, in the sense that the metal ions can be coordinated with dendrimers, and can be used to generate oxygen for achieving high performance PDT. PAMAM dendrimers themselves induce oxidative stress by producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) resulting in apoptosis and DNA damage. Rodriguez et al. reported that dendrimers conjugated with ALA (5-aminolaevulinic acid) can be efficiently utilized in superficial cancers as well as for vascular PDT [37]. They carried out study to infuse ALA for activation of protoporphyrin-IX already present in the body, and then the activated porphyrin was incorporated for PDT applications. It was already known that dendrimers conjugated with ALA enhanced the porphyrin synthesis. In this work, it was concluded that even low concentration of ALA dendrimers could synthesize higher amount of porphyrin as compared to ALA itself. This was attributed to the fact that the amino groups of the ALA molecules could protonated in vivo, thus conferring good water solubility on the dendrimer.

On the other hand, the major hindrance of tumor hypoxia can be overcome by employing metal ion coordinated dendrimers. These systems act as nanoenzymes that respond to low pH and H2O2 in the TME to generate O2 for achieving satisfactory in vivo PDT performance. In a particular carrier, the amino groups containing PAMAM dendrimers modified with polyethylene glycol-cholesterol were coordinated with manganese dioxide which in turn generates oxygen thus counteracting on the hypoxic tumor microenvironment for an effective PDT [36]. Moreover, dendrimers can release cytochrome C upon co-localization with mitochondria, leading to disruption of mitochondrial membrane and disruption of cell viability. In this manner, PAMAM dendrimers themselves induce oxidative stress by producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) resulting in apoptosis and DNA damage [38]. The presence of hydrophobic pockets on the certain dendrimer systems accounts for reasonable encapsulation and segregation of PSs resulting into their improved water solubility and enhanced PDT.

An important strategy for achieving an effective PDT is by improvising the selectivity of the photosensitizers via its uptake only in the tumor cells and not in the healthy cells. One of the best possibility of achieving this is by conjugating the PS to biomolecules like small ligands, peptides, nucleic acids, lipids etc that can efficiently penetrate cells by proteins/receptors overexpression on the cancer cells [39]. It is also noteworthy that the anomalies caused in PDT owing to the hypoxic tumor microenvironment can also be overcome by incorporation of biomolecules to design nanomaterials for PDT [40]. Interestingly, photosensitizers have also been conjugated with hemoglobin to serve as oxygen carrying carriers for efficiently generating an oxidative stress [41]. DNA has the capability to encapsulate PSs within its G-quadruplex nanostructure. DNA in various forms like nanogels, nanosponges etc have been encapsulated with some of the commonly employed PS like chlorine e 6 and zinc phthalocyanine for an enhanced sensitization [42]. DNA hybrids containing mesoporous silica have been designed for an enhanced PDT. The material exists as nanogels, having a core of virus-like mesoporous silica nanoparticles (VMSN) providing a self-assembling programmable DNA hydrogel layer. This structure is then impregnated with hemin and zinc phthalocyanine by base pairing to act as an oxygen supplement and for generation of singlet oxygen, respectively. Lipid nanocarriers have also been explored as drug carriers for skin cancer therapy due to their ability of deep tissue penetration. This property has been employed by Fadel et al. to synthesize curcumin loaded, lipid derived PEGylated nanocarriers for targeting skin cancer [43]. The carriers demonstrated an effective to cure the epidermal ulceration and necrosis in rat model under the influence of PDT.

Various glycopolymers are also observed to be effective for improving the efficacy of delivering PSs for instance a galactose containing polymer bearing porphyrin as the PS has demonstrated effective toxicity towards human liver cancer cell lines.

The conjugation of PS with the biomolecule renders water solubility to the PS and the porphyrin at the core aids in the production of singlet oxygen species. Thus subsequently, lipid based systems can improvise PDT by an enhanced internalization of the carriers and promising biocompatibility as well as due to its capability to effectively deliver PS as observed via in-vitro and in-vivo studies. Some the recently employed nanosystems for inducing oxidative stress have been mentioned in Table 1 .

Despite the developments of new materials for PDT researchers are have been working over the past few years to enhance the efficacy of PDT. The strategy involved to achieve this goal involves enhancement in the selectivity of a treatment module via development of new targeted PS or nanoparticles. This has led to an advent of various advanced techniques for PDT summarized in Table 2 .

Another strategy can be improving the selectivity of X-ray induced PDT is by delivering PDT inducing molecules in close proximity to carcinogenic tissues [58]. Ongoing work by various groups involves the use of PS (protoporphyrin, metalloporphyrins, dyes like methylene blue, acridine orange etc.) and nanoparticles (TiO2, ZnS:Ag, CeF3, CdSe, Ce3+-doped lanthanum(III) fluoride (LaF3:Ce3+) etc.) that can be stimulated by X-rays for a better performance [59,60]. It is interesting to note the developments of PS loaded metallic nanoparticles as X-ray excitable nanovehicles. Such research on X-ray induced PDT applications was pioneered by Chen and co-workers wherein nanoparticles were employed to emit scintillating luminescence for the activation of the photosensitizers [56]. Chang and co-workers have developed a scintillating lanthanide loaded silica nanoparticle with a core − shell − shell arrangement [NaLuF4:Gd (35 %),Eu(15 %)@NaLuF4:Gd(40 %)@NaLuF4:Gd(35 %),Tb(15 %)] [61]. This material is capable of being excited by an X-ray irradiation source and has been loaded with rose bengal (RB) dye that functions as photosensitizer. It was also observed that a PS remains non-toxic unless it receives exposure light of suitable wavelength. Similarly, Liu et. al., have worked on synthesis of water-soluble LaF3:Ce,Tb and LaF3 :Tb nanoparticles for generation of x-ray luminescence in such a way that there is a reduction in the wastage of generated energy to solvent-particle interactions for further enhanced treatment [62].

The light activation generates ROS/radicals that can not only cause death of cancer cells but also disrupt blood vessels and stimulate immune cells. Such cell killing mechanisms were found to be the reason for lowered therapeutic effects in cells that are not directly exposed to light. Thus combining nontoxic organic molecules and light for ROS generation can aid a selective treatment [63].

Upon irradiation these nanovehicles emit visible light at 543 nm stimulating the dye via energy transfer from the nanoparticles. This energy transfer causes an efficient PDT leading to the death of 2 different breast cancer cell lines (MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7).

Interestingly Lang et al., have developed a new generation of radiosensitizers based on octahedral molybdenum cluster complexes (Mo6) [64]. These complexes can directly emit singlet oxygen species post X-ray exposure which eliminates the possibility of energy losses during energy transfer from the scintillating nanoparticles to the PSs in conventional systems. The material demonstrated an effective elimination of HeLa (cervical cancer cell lines) and MRC (fibroblasts) cells due to effective radio sensitization at clinically pertinent X-ray doses of 1, 2 and 4 Gy. The efficacy and safety of PDTX for the treatment of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and melanoma has been established via various in-vitro and in-vivo experiments by the administration of Cu-cysteamine nanoparticles as well [65].

The Microwave induced PDT aims similar to PTDX in terms of deeper tissue penetration. Microwaves have the ability to propagate via all types of tissues as well as nonmetallic materials, thus better outcomes are expected via this approach. The use of microwave thermotherapy in combination with PDT and chemotherapy dates back to 1980s. This technique works mechanistically via a pathway wherein dyplastic and photo damaged cells die upon heating effect/hyperthermia caused by microwave exposure [58,56]. Studies over the years have established that the efficacy of PDT increases upon using microwave stimulation. A variety of microwave susceptible nano sized materials like metallic nanoparticles, single-walled carbon nanotubes, and iron oxide formulations have been explored for heat generation. This is because; NPs have the potential to render the tumor leakier thus further enhancing the PDT. For instance copper cysteamine (Cu-Cy) nanoparticles synthesized by Yao and co-workers have been known to exert a microwave-induced PDT via singlet oxygen generation for release of ROS [66]. It is noteworthy that not only microwave but Cu-Cy nanoparticles are also capable of inducing cancer therapy by sono-sensitization via use of ultrasound [67]. The animal studies demonstrated a significant tumor regression on the xenograft of 4T1 cancer cells when they were treated with 0.75 mg kg−1 of Cu–Cy nanoparticles and subjected to ultrasound. Further, TiO2 nanoparticles possessing good photocatalytic activity has also been explored by Chu et. al., for the generation of ROS upon exposure to microwave-induced plasmonic effect [68]. Interestingly, these particles demonstrated an enhanced cytotoxicity on osteosarcoma UMR-106 cells which thus establishing the effectiveness of TiO2 nanoparticles for microwave induced radical cancer therapy. Some quaternary chalcogenides of the Cu2-II-IV-VI4 family including Cu2ZnSnS4 and Cu2ZnSnSe4 possessing high absorption coefficients also have been used recently for similar application. The Cu2ZnSnS4 nanocrystals were observed to improve the heating effect of microwaves to increase the temperature at tumor site which is sufficient to destroy the tumor within 5 min of microwave irradiation. Interestingly, quantum dots in graphitic phase have the capability to produce singlet O species upon microwave irradiation. This system has been explored to treat osteosarcoma in rat cells which is one of the difficult forms of cancer to treat [69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phage and stem cell based photosensitizer delivery for PDT enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: The application of an ideal PS plays a pivotal role to achieving desired results in PDT. Additionally the suitable PS should bear amphiphilicity that can be tuned in biological surroundings. Advances have been made to make them targeting in nature with adjustable retention times for clearance from the body once the treatment is completed [58]. This challenge has been met by researchers by finding new avenues for selectively targeting PS to the tumor site. One of the most intriguing delivery vehicle for PS explored is bacteriophage. Bacteriophages are excellent vectors for PS delivery because of their low cost, safety, physical stability and compatibility with host (Fig. 3a). Phages have been safely experimented on animals as well as humans for treatment of bacterial infections. Phage coat proteins forming virus-like particles have also been used as vaccines [70]. These successful human trials have encouraged its application for cancer therapy. In a study carried out by Mao et al., PS was conjugated to phage to prepare a complex and it PDT efficiency was evaluated [71]. A phage corresponding to SKBR-3 breast cancer cell targeting was selected. The complex resembling a nanowire successfully acted as a targeting vehicle, delivering photosensitizer to the desired site Fig. 3b.

Stem cells on the other hand have also assisted in targeted delivery of PSs, the mechanism of their efficacy is linked to various chemokines like epidermal growth factor, and plate based growth factors, stromal growth factors that have affinity for tumor [72]. Results demonstrate that this strong affinity of stem cells assists them to accumulate at the breast tumors upon injecting via tail vein. The growing demand for application of stem cells as carriers is owing to the fact that they can be easily isolated from the patient’s bone marrow, modified as per requirement and again transplanted in the patient for the required purpose. Mao and coworkers have synthesized mesenchymal stem cells loaded with PS conjugated mesoporous silica nanoparticles for treatment of rats bearing breast cancer.

The formulation upon injection was found to retain in the tumor and generated ROS which assisted in tumor inhibition suggesting the efficacy of the strategy. Similarly stem cells have been conjugated with various other nanomaterials like graphene oxide, fullerenes etc to confer them ROS up regulating capabilities for efficacious PDT [2].

Photosensitization caused by the therapy leads to oxidation of cellular macromolecules like lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids thus damaging the subcellular structures. At the cellular level, photosensitizer induces an oxidative stress that increases expression of some early-response genes along with cellular stress proteins, glucose-regulated proteins and heme oxygenase. Protein phosphorylation is the signaling pathway that causes PDT mediated gene activation. In a study by Gomer and group shows that photo sensitizer-mediated oxidative stress is known to activate the heat shock factor as well as increase heat shock protein (HSP-70 mRNA) [74]. The major limitations for determining the reactive oxygen species (ROS) based cell death is, ascertaining the type of ROS produced, the nature of intracellular ROS signaling, ROS localization and establishing a relation between them. There lies a possibility that ROS may induce the release of both transcription factors/activators and genes associated with tumor suppression.

The mechanism for selective and specific killing of tumor cells by ROS generated from various materials remains unclear and hence attempts are being made by researchers to unfold them. Winyard and co-workers have worked on deciphering the mechanism for cell death mediated by ROS generation from graphene based platforms (Fig. 4b) [75]. They elaborated that graphene initially undergoes internalization via clathrin/caveolar-mediated endocytosis, that is based on its size, shape, and surface functionalizations. Internalization can also occur via other methods like phagocytosis, macro pinocytosis, and pinocytosis.

The internalization assists the nanoplatform to escape the lysosomal, vesicle, and non-vesicle secretions. The fate of graphene further is decided by its interaction with plasma membrane and this is also a deciding factor for an efficient PDT. A sequence of events may occur post this interaction like adsorption or incorporation of graphene onto the cell surface.

On the extracellular matrix, the surface functionalizations on the graphene surface assist to alter protein’s tertiary structure and cause it to malfunction. The nature of malfunctions occurring still remains unresolved and requires detailed studies for future insight. Once the graphene based nanodevice enters the cells the production of ROS upon light irradiation exerts an oxidative stress on the cancer cells. The generated oxidative stress causes a various anomalies in the cells like loss of cell function, damage of mitochondria and beginning of lipid peroxidation. There is also a possibility that nucleic acids undergo covalent chemical modification and the strands of DNA cleave. Further the activation of transcription factors induces gene expression via signal transduction causing modulations in inflammation that leads to toxicity and ultimately cell death [5].

Similar mechanisms have been evidenced NIR-mediated PDT using up-conversion nanoparticles. It was observed that cell death is induced via a mitochondrial apoptosis pathway process which occurs due to expression of caspase 3 in tumor tissue being upregulated under oxidative stress generated by PDT [2]. Owing to the evolution of PDT as an important facet of cancer therapeutic modality, unraveling the mechanisms involved will further help to assist in more efficient new nanoplatform fabrication strategies. Also localization of ROS generating nanomedicine in the cancer specific regions is also has a great significance in elevating the performance of treatment with PDT.

Even though PDT has been clinically applied to treat various tumors, there are certain challenges for classical PS based PDT, such as poor water solubility, insufficient penetration of light in tissue and non-specific selectivity of tumor tissues. Employing hybrid nonmaterial can address these issues at large. Several hybrid nanomaterials have been designed to assist efficacious oxidative stress mediated PDT that can also support multimodal cancer treatment. Outstanding properties of nanomaterials like facile modifications, surface functionalization and conjugation of targeting moieties have further improvised the accumulation and specificity of PSs. The combination of PDT with other therapies like PTT, fluorescence imaging, drug delivery etc. has also emerged as a safe and effective strategy to achieve desirable effects while reducing side effects. However, despite of its rapid curative effect, minimal invasiveness, and evasion of drug resistance, the application of PDT is a subject to sufficient rigorous clinical trials and in vivo compatibility. The intravenously injected nano materials may induce severe toxicity depending on its size incompatibility with cells of vital organs and administered dose. Therefore, understanding interaction of such materials with normal and cancer cells and investigating its behavior with hematological factors such as RBC and WBC is at paramount.

Primary biological studies have been reported for nanohybrids carrying PS. Metal-organic framework (MOF) nanoparticle developed for PDT and anticancer therapy performed optimized in vivo fate, biodistribution and plasma pharmacokinetics. The histological examination showed good biocompatibility. Satisfactory in vivo PDT performance has been monitored for nanoenzymes based on dendrimer showing tumor accumulation performance. PAMAM dendrimers possess neuroinflammation-targeting capability and longer blood circulation time which served to enhance efficacy of dendrimer-9-amino-minocycline conjugate. Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) owe high specific area, declined silanol density, as well as large pore size which are considered as favorable properties in terms of biocompatibility. Most of the reports denoted that MSNs possess low toxicity under biological environment. MSNs toxicity in endothelial cells was correlated to its particle size stating that particles with size greater than 100 nm have low cytotoxicity. Surface charge on MSNs was stated as another variable to project cytotoxicity, where positive charge displays the lower cytotoxicity than bare MSNs. Furthermore, surface modification of MSNs by PEGylation was reported to reduce non specific attachment to serum proteins, which can lead to increased blood compatibility. Nano sized hybrid materials based on Graphine QDs and Ag nanoparticles were proposed for PDT application. This material showed antimicrobial efficacy against E. coli and S. aureus in vitro and remarkable therapeutic effect in vivo using animal models. Other such QDs namely Molybdenum disulfide QDs and Manganese-doped carbon quantum dots (Mn-CQDs) were tested for in vitro experiments which showed acceptable biocompatibility and high cellular uptake. However, satisfactory clinical evidences on the biocompatibility of PS conjugated nano hybrids and their safety assessment in context of regulatory requirements are the need of the hour. Detailed investigation on these factors could assist in understanding the flaws of such PDT agents and pave a way to improve its suitability for further commercialization and application.
